segments.text: Out of all the different kinds of solar
systems, ours is the rarest. Simulations
and surveys of other planets tell us
that this one is by far the most common,
where all the planets are about the same
size and mass, like peas in a pod. So,
how did our solar system get like this
with all the smaller ones closer in and
all the bigger ones farther out? The
leading theory has to do with Jupiter.
We think it was the first planet to form
out of the swirling clouds of gas and
dust around our sun. And it got pulled
inwards by gas plowing through and
clearing out material. And then came
Saturn. And it inched close enough to
Jupiter that the two became synced. And
that kept Jupiter from moving in any
closer than here, about where Mars is
today. They played this tugofwar with
the rest of the dust disc, eventually
reversing their direction and pulling
them back out. But in the inner solar
system, only crumbs were left to form
smaller planets like Earth and Mars.
Yeah, we're cosmic crumbs. Sorry. And
we've looked around at lots of other
solar systems, and it seems like we're
the odd one out. To learn more,
subscribe.
